risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization will be performed, by a research assistance, via a computer generated sequence in blocks of 10 recruited subjects. The allocation ratio into each arm will be 1:1. The randomization will be stratified for age into 2 groups: less than 35 years old and greater or equal to 35 years old." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random sequence with blocked and stratified randomization, which is a proper random allocation method. Although the document is a protocol and not the final results paper, the stated method is sufficient to judge sequence generation as low risk.

Quote: "Randomization will be performed, by a research assistance, via a computer generated sequence in blocks of 10 recruited subjects." Comment: This reports sequence generation and who performed randomization, but it does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or sequentially numbered identical containers) preventing the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment. The mention that all capsules were indistinguishable and that patients/caregivers were blinded does not itself establish allocation concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This trial will have 2 arms. All capsules will be indistinguishable from each other.(1)Placebo tablet taken once, 30 minutes pre-ET.(2)20 mg immediate release nifedipine tablet taken once, 30 minutes pre-ET" and "Neither patients nor caregivers will be aware of the allocation. All clinical staff will remain blinded to allocation of therapy until the statistical data base is cleaned and locked." Comment: Participants and care providers were explicitly blinded, and the placebo/capsule matching suggests successful masking. The main outcomes (implantation, clinical pregnancy, live birth, miscarriage) could in principle be influenced by knowledge of allocation, but the study methods indicate adequate blinding of both participants and personnel, so performance bias is low.

Quote: "Neither patients nor caregivers will be aware of the allocation of therapy until the statistical data base is cleaned and locked. All clinical staff will remain blinded to allocation of therapy until the statistical data base is cleaned and locked." Comment: The study explicitly reports blinding of patients, caregivers, and clinical staff, which covers outcome assessment. The main outcomes were implantation rate, clinical pregnancy rate, live birth, miscarriage, and pregnancy/neonatal outcomes, all of which are largely objective and based on serum hCG, ultrasound, delivery records, or neonatal records. Therefore, knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to influence outcome measurement.

Summary: The protocol states that analysis would be by intention to treat and that every reasonable effort would be made to maintain participant participation; the study report/review characteristics show 93 randomized participants (47 placebo, 46 nifedipine) with no reported differential loss to follow-up or missing outcome data signal. Comment: Although explicit per-arm attrition numbers are not provided here, the available information suggests mostly complete follow-up with no indication of imbalanced or outcome-related dropout, so attrition bias is unlikely.
